summary: Jeff Atwood recounts how early in his career, a mentor named Bill O'Neil taught him the crucial lesson that writing code is only half the job—testing it ruthlessly is the other half. Through painful code reviews where his work was systematically demolished, Atwood learned that most bugs aren't in the code logic but in failing to anticipate the countless ways users will break your application. He argues that every programmer must embrace being their own worst enemy, actively trying to break their own code before others do. The post catalogs common 'falsehoods programmers believe' about names, time, geography, and addresses to illustrate just how many edge cases exist in real-world programming.
key_points: The truly difficult part of programming isn't writing code—it's handling the thousands of ways things can go wrong, most of them user-related
Professional programmers must learn to be their own worst enemy, deliberately breaking their UI and code before users do
Real-world data is messy: names, dates, times, addresses, and geography all have countless edge cases that violate programmer assumptions
Having a mentor or colleague who ruthlessly tests your code is invaluable for professional growth
If you don't break your own code, users will—and they'll either walk away or create support tickets
money_quotes: What I clearly had no idea about is testing my code.
I slowly, painfully learned that the truly difficult part of coding is dealing with the thousands of ways things can go wrong with your application at any given time – most of them user related.
If you are ever lucky enough to work with one, you should have a very, very healthy fear of professional testers. They are terrifying.
A key turning point in every professional programmer's working life is when you realize you are your own worst enemy, and the only way to mitigate that threat is to embrace it.
Act like your own worst enemy. Break your UI. Break your code. Do terrible things to your software.
You are tester zero. This is your responsibility.
A good programmer knows they have to do terrible things to their code.
If you don't, I guarantee you other people will, and when they do, they will either walk away or create a support ticket. I'm not sure which is worse.
key_insight: Every programmer must embrace being their own worst enemy by ruthlessly testing their code, because real users will find every edge case you missed.
